The work and the approach
  

   

  What I do
  
     I work at the intersection of public data and policy decisions — taking 
        administrative and fiscal records that governments produce but rarely 
        interrogate, and turning them into evidence that decision-makers can 
        act on.
    
      My focus is Nepal's intergovernmental fiscal system: how federal 
      resources flow to provincial and local governments, whether that flow 
      reflects stated policy priorities, and where the gaps between allocation 
      logic and actual outcomes are widest. I work across health, education, 
      agricultural policy, and electoral analytics — not because these are 
      separate interests, but because the same fiscal architecture shapes all 
      of them.
    
    
      The clients and organisations I work with need more than charts — they 
      need a clear answer to a hard question. My work is oriented toward that 
      answer, not toward the data itself.
    
  

  How I work
  
     
      All analysis is produced in R and published as reproducible Quarto 
      documents. I work directly from primary sources — Ministry of Finance 
      budget data, MoFAGA conditional grant records, Election Commission 
      results, and household survey microdata — rather than aggregated 
      secondary sources.
      
    
      I am especially interested in subnational variation: the patterns that 
      disappear when data is aggregated to the national level. Nepal's 
      federal system creates 753 units of observation. Most analysis uses 
      seven provinces. The most important findings tend to live in the 
      difference between those two scales.
    
  

  Background
  
    
      I am based in Kathmandu. My work on Nepal's fiscal decentralisation 
      has covered health workforce financing, basic education conditional 
      grants, out-of-pocket health expenditure, and electoral constituency 
      analysis. Earlier work includes trade policy — specifically 
      Nepal's sugarcane sector and import duty structures.
    
    
      I am available for consulting engagements, research collaborations, 
      and analytical commissions. If your organisation needs someone who 
      can move between government budget documents and actionable 
      policy recommendations — and produce the analysis in between — 
      I would be glad to talk.

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
text: Ministry of Health and Population is one of the biggest spender of federal Budget in Nepal. A significant portion of its budget constitutes conditional grants to 753 local governments. This data brief is an attempt to analyse and explore patterns and trends of budgetory allocation of conditional grants for salaries and allowances of health personel working at local level.

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
section: Lets Look into budget allocation for “प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य सेवा”
text:
Lets Look into budget allocation for “प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य सेवा”
The budget allocated under “प्राथमिक स्वस्थ्य सेवा” is the expenses to be incurred in salaries and various allowances for the health workers at health facilities at Local Level. The government guideline1 defines this (ie प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य सेवा) Budget Sub Heading (No. 3700102) as following:






TipDefination of Budget Sub Heading No 3700102



“स्थानिय तहका स्वास्थ्य शाखा, स्वस्थ्य चौकी, प्राथमिक स्वस्थ्य केन्द्र, अस्पताल तथा आयुर्वेद औषधालयहरुमा कार्यरत स्थायी कर्मचारीहरुको स्वीकृत दरवन्दी अनुसारको तलव, महंगी भत्ता र पोषाक भत्ता”


The Figure 3 illustrates the distribution of budget allocation per LG (values in Lakhs) for the Budget Subheading 37001012 (ie प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य सेवा). The text in red with the names of the LG represents the outlier2.








Figure 3: Boxplot for total budget at each local level





As per the IQR critereon of identifying outlier, there exists 28 LG which are categorised as outlier; for the purpose of this data exercise only 4 (extreme outliers) observations are considered as outliers which includes budget allocated at Pokhara Metro, Birgunj Metro, Godavari Municipality and Bharatpur Metro.




Table 1: List of LG which is identified as extreme outliers

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
section: Proportion of budget allocation - Province wise
text:
Proportion of budget allocation - Province wise








Figure 4: Province wise proportion of budget allocation




Lets Look into what portion of population live in each Provinces








Figure 5: Proportion of total allocated budget against total population




The Figure 5 attempts to compare the budget allocation against the population in each province. The budget-to-population ratio appears balanced, except in Karnali and Gandaki provinces. Gandaki Province receives 12.21% of the total budget allocated under Budget Subhead 3700102, despite comprising only 8.42% of the total population. It would be interesting to explore whether there is a correlation between budget allocation and performance in various health indicators across provinces. For instance, how does Gandaki—where per capita budget allocation is high—compare to Bagmati, which has a lower per capita budget allocation relative to other provinces?
It is important to note that Budget Subhead No. 3700102 covers health-related human resource costs at the local level. Ideally, the budget allocation should be proportional to the actual size of the health workforce in each local unit. Assuming a direct proportional relationship between budget allocation and the number of health workers, Gandaki and Karnali provinces would have the highest number of per capita health workers at the local level. Therefore, analyzing the relationship between per capita spending on local-level health workers and actual health outcomes would be a valuable exercise in optimizing government spending and improving efficiency at the outcome level.

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
text:
Budget Allocation in heat map !








Figure 6: Heat map of budget allocation per LG on Primary Health Care





Figure 6 above doesnt plot (in order not to skew the data) four LG identified as outlier above (ie Pokhara Metro, Birgunj Metro, Godavari Municipality and Bharatpur Metro).

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
text:
Equity Analysis
Lets make an attempt to evaluate whether the budget allocation is proportional to population size accross local level governments in Nepal.








Figure 7: Simple Linear Model of Population and total budget allocation for each local level





Figure 7 attempts to visualise the relationship between population (in 1000s) and total budget allocation (in 10 million). A linear regression line with a confidence interval is included to show the general trend between population size and budget allocation.
Following are the key observations based on the chart:

Positive Correlation: There is a weak positive correlation between population and budget allocation, as indicated by the regression line.
The low R² value: the low R² value (0.11) suggests that population alone does not strongly explain budget allocation. Only 11% of the variation in the budget is explained by population alone. Other factors (eg policy decisions, political considerations) could be at play.
The Curious Case of Six Metros: Pokhara, Birgunj and Bharatpur recieve higher budget than predicted by the linear model based on the population size. Similarly, Lalitpur, Biratnagar and Kathmandu recieves significantly low amount of budget than prediction. This too further substantiates that (in this case Metros) population alone is not the main driver of budget allocation.
Explaining Linear Model and Coefficient The regression equation shown in the plot is:\[y = 2.43 \times 10^{+7} + 80.5x\]
The intercept \[\beta_o = 2.43  \times  10^{+7}\] represents the value of Total Budget if the population is zero. In this context we can presume that this value (24.3 million) is the bare minimum cost estimate for human resources regardless of the population.
The slope \[\beta_1 = 80.5\] means for every additional 1000 people, the total budget increases by 80.5 million (or 8.05 Crores)

Lets see if there are any Patterns at Provinces








Figure 8: Linear Model (Total Budget V Population) at each local level for each provinces





Figure 8 illustrates relationship between total budget allocated and population along with a simple linear model for each provinces. Following observations can be made:

In Gandaki and Madhesh Province, the R² is singinificantly higher (at 0.52) than other Provinces. This implies that there is moderately strong positive correlation between population and total budget allocated in these Provinces. This also means that 52% of variance in budget allocation can be explained by the population.
In Koshi and Sudur Paschim, the R² value is < 0.01, this means population cannot be considered a predictor for total budget allocation.

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
section: Lets consider Budget Per Capita (for Primary Health Services at Local Level)
text:
Lets consider Budget Per Capita (for Primary Health Services at Local Level)








Figure 9: Heat map representing Per capita budget allocation for each LG





Province-wiseLG-TypePer Capita household










Figure 10: Per Capita total Budget Allocation in 753 Local Governments of Nepal for Primary Health Services















Figure 11: Per Capita and Per Household Budget Allocation at different LG Types















Figure 12: Per household total Budget Allocation in 753 Local Governments of Nepal for Primary Health Services








The Figure 10 clearly shows that Gandaki Province exhibits the highest variation in per capita budget allocation, while Madhesh Province has the least variation, with a generally lower per capita budget. Similarly, Figure 11 highlights that Rural Municipalities (Gaunpalika) have the greatest variation in both per capita and per household budget allocation. This could be attributed to differences in geographical terrain and the relatively smaller populations in some rural municipalities, which may explain the presence of outliers and a portion of the overall variation within this category.
Moreover, when considering Per Household Budget Allocation instead of per capita, Figure 12 demonstrates an even higher variation across almost all provinces, particularly in Karnali, Sudurpaschim, Bagmati, and Gandaki, compared to per capita allocation.

Population v per capita budget








Figure 13: Per Capita budget V Population




As expected Figure 13 clearly illustrates the lower per capita budget for Local Level with higher population (Metro) and higher for Rural Municipalities. It is interesting to note that the Pokhara Municipality which recives exceptionally higher volume of budget under this heading also has relatively lower per capita budget. However, if other metro such as Kathmandu is to match the budget of Pokhara (based on population) there will be significant increase in budget demand.
The higest variation in data is attributed to the Rural Municipalities. In the following figure only Rural Municipalities is plotted Figure 14.








Figure 14: Per Capita budget V population for Rural municipalities in all Provinces




The higher Per Capita Budget are in the mountainous Local Level mostly of Gandaki Province (Mustang and Manang) and of Karnali Province. If we are to discard any Local Level with more than 3000 per capita budget (assuming these are mostly mountanous LG with disproportionately lower population) the plot will appear as follow:

Rural MunicipalityMunicipality










Figure 15: Per Capita budget V population for Rural municipalities in all Provinces (for per capita budget less than 3000 NPR)














Figure 16: Per Capita budget V population for municipalities in all Provinces







Figure 15 provides a better prospective into the relationship between the population and per capita budget; with increase in volume of population the variation around the trendline seems to be reduced. However, such reduction aint as encouraging; for example Joshipur Rural Municipality of Sudur Psachim with population 37 thousands and 2 hundred people have a per capita budget of NPR 393 per person, while another Palhinandan rural municipality with similar population has NPR 877 per capita budget. Similar phenomenon can be observed among Municipalities as illustrated in Figure 16

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level
text:
Key Takeaways

The primary health service (ie प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य सेवा) comprises the highest budgetary allocation (20.64 billion) among conditional loan provided which provided to Local Level for health related expenses. The government guideline makes it clear that the expenses under this heading is related to salary and allowances for the human resources working at the heath facilities at local level. The data exercise makes it very clear that budgetary allocation under this subheading is not strong function of population
Subnational level health indicator (LG level) could be used to evaluate the efficiency of budgetary allocation.
The policy questions :

The data suggests that there is a high variations in the number of health personnel employed at local level (everything remaining constant, budget volume is fundamentally a function of number of health personnel). What determines the number of health personnel at local level? Should the population it caters to be a general predictor of the number of health personnel and vis-à-vis total budget allocation?
What other variables, other than population, should determine the budgetary allocation (or number of health personnel) at Local Level? Or is it such that the local levels with lower per capita budgetary allocation are “healthy patches of population” that ought to require less investment from the state ?

title: Demystifying Conditional Grants for Salaries and allowances of health personel at local level

title: Financing Basic Education- From a lens of student-teacher ratio
text:
This exercise is an attempt to analyse conditional grants as allocated by the Federal Government of Nepal for education at Local Level. Pursuant to the constitution of Nepal, the basic education falls within the power of the Local Level Governments.
The legacy of pre-federal education system still holds a strong grip in the Education Sector in Nepal including those in the Local Level - which have constitutional power of Basic Education. This means, the federal government have had hands-on approach in matters of resource allocation for the publicly funded school at local level, via conditional grants. The conditional grants for education targeted at publicly funded schools at local level comprises a significant chunk of total government spending for any single program.
While there exists significant and acute [horizontal] fiscal imbalance among local level governments to run and operate their constitutional mandate of basic education, which makes it only imperative for federal government to intervene and correct the imbalance. However, the modus operandi of federal government of Nepal in allocating resources to counter the pervasive fiscal imbalance in Education Sector in Local Level has not been within the spirit of constitutional mandate. In practice public education system is being run as a federal program; for example, in the Fiscal Year 2080-81, the federal budget had 18 different activity code amounting to 135 billion rupees for 753 Local Level Government under conditional grants for education. The level of micro-management in resource allocation of education sector is not only against the spirit of constitution and federalism, it also adds administrative cost to federal government.
This exercise makes an attempt to analyse data on budget and school to understand budgetary allocation under conditional grant from federal government to local government which could be used to identify possible alternative methods to decentralize the process such that Local Level government are empowered to exercise their constitutional power of basic education.

title: Financing Basic Education- From a lens of student-teacher ratio
section: Student - teacher ratio in heat map
text:
Student - teacher ratio in heat map








Figure 4: Heatmap of student teacher ratio at Basic Level (Grade 1 to 8) at all Local Level





The heat map Figure 4 above, clearly illustrates disproportionately high student teacher ratio in Madhesh Province. Lets examine features of Madhesh Province in regard to student teacher ratio.
